# Chapter One: The Released

The coffee had gone cold hours ago. Vera didn't notice until she lifted the cup and the liquid hit her tongue like small percussion against her teeth.

Small mercies. Cold coffee meant she'd been sitting long enough to forget she was waiting.

Outside the porthole, the Flotilla creaked and shifted. These weren't proper ships—just a hundred different vessels lashed together over the years, grafted into one sprawling mass that drifted with the currents and the tides and whatever direction the tideglass-powered anchors decided to pull. The smell of it came through the salt-crusted window: fish and smoke and the chemical bite of the tideglass generators, underneath it all the organic rot of a community that existed at the margins of everything.

She'd been in the Flotilla four years. Four years of waking up in windowless rooms, of keeping her back to walls, of sleeping with one hand under the pillow where the sap waited. Four years of watching Mira from a distance, of the letters that came every other week in Mira's careful hand, of visits that felt like rehearsals for a life she wasn't allowed to have.

The letter was sitting on the counter where it had been since morning.

She didn't need to read it again. She'd memorized the words the first time, then the second, then a third just to be certain. Mira was coming for the summer. Mira was finally coming to stay. Three weeks in the Flotilla, two weeks in the lower city where Vera had rented a room with a window that faced the water, a whole summer of being something other than a ghost haunting the edges of her daughter's life.

Important things happened on Tuesdays in books. In stories, the letter promising change arrived and the story began. But this was Tuesday and nothing had happened yet, and Vera knew better than to trust the shape of narratives.

She got up. Her knees protested—another side effect of the bloodforge, the way it had calcified something in her joints and left her forty pounds lighter than she should have been. At thirty-seven she moved like a woman twenty years older, and at thirty-seven she looked like a woman twenty years older still.

The mirror above the basin showed what it always showed: a face that had been weathered past recognition, lines carved deep around the mouth and eyes, dark hair going silver at the temples. The crimson teardrop behind her left ear had faded to a bruised purple. The thirty-seven marks counting her kills—she counted them every morning without meaning to—had blurred into a single dark smear, like someone had tried to wash her history away and only partially succeeded.

She splashed water on her face. It didn't help.

Three days. Mira would arrive in three days on the morning ferry from the Upper Arches, and Vera would be there at the dock, trying to remember how to be a person instead of a collection of old habits and older regrets. She'd practiced the words. *I've missed you. Tell me about your summer. What do you want to do first?* They sounded like lines from a play she hadn't been given the script for.

The knock came at the door.

Vera's hand went to the sap in her pocket before the rest of her caught up. Old instincts. She was retired, she reminded herself. She was a woman who lived in the Flotilla now, who fixed nets and mended sails and kept her head down. She didn't know anyone here well enough for them to knock.

She opened the door anyway.

The man on the other side was tall and thin, wearing a Dockward Council uniform that sat wrong on his frame, like he'd borrowed it from someone with different proportions. His face was young but his eyes were old, the kind of eyes that came from seeing too much and choosing not to look away.

"Mrs. Salt?" he asked.

That was the name on the Flotilla registry. Vera Salt, widow, net-mender. No family, no history, no connection to anything that had happened before she washed up on the driftwood edge of Verath's economy.

"Yes."

"I have a delivery." He held out a box wrapped in oilcloth, the kind they used for things that couldn't get wet. The box was small enough to fit in one hand, dark wood, no markings. "Signed for by the sender. Someone called—" He consulted a ledger, the gesture too careful, too deliberate. "Someone called Salt-Hand."

The name hit her like a blade between the ribs.

She didn't reach for it. Not yet. Four years of building a life out of nothing, four years of pretending she was someone else, and someone had found her. Someone had signed that name on a delivery chit and sent it to her door.

"Who gave you this?"

"Harbor Authority transfer. It came up from the Undercroft." His eyes flickered—nervous, she thought, though it could have been a trick of the thin light. "I just deliver. I don't ask."

He set the box on the narrow porch and stepped back, already turning away. The uniform rode up on one shoulder as he moved. Wrong size. Definitely borrowed.

"Wait," Vera said.

He stopped. Didn't turn around.

"Who signed for it at the Undercroft?"

"I told you. Harbor Authority transfer. The chit says Salt-Hand." A pause, weighted. "It also says the sender paid triple rate for rush delivery. To ensure it arrived today."

Today. Not in three days when Mira came. Today, while Mira was still on the ferry, still hours away, still safe.

The man walked down the narrow plank that served as a walkway between floating homes. Vera watched him go, counting the steps until he reached the main dock, until he transferred onto a boat-cart that would carry him back to Verath proper. Counting was something she did without meaning to. She'd stopped counting the dead years ago, but she still counted everything else. Steps. Breaths. The small mechanics of a world that kept moving whether she was ready or not.

She picked up the box.

The weight was wrong. Too heavy for the size, and the weight was distributed unevenly, like something shifted inside when she tilted it. She could feel the tideglass thrum against her scarred palm—faint but unmistakable. Someone had packed this with tideglass. Someone who knew what it was, what it could do, what it was worth.

Vera carried the box inside and set it on the counter beside Mira's letter.

The cold coffee sat there like an accusation. The letter from the Covenant's Housing Office, the one confirming Mira's transfer request, the one that had arrived two weeks ago with the official seal and the waiting period already waived. Mira had written in the margins, her handwriting slanting hard to the right, as if the words were trying to escape the page: *Mom, I get to come home.*

Vera had read those words forty-seven times. She'd counted.

She unwrapped the oilcloth.

Inside the dark wood box, nestled in straw that smelled of salt and copper, was a severed finger.

Not just a finger. A finger wearing a ring—a small silver band with an opal set in the center, the stone catching light in a way that should have been beautiful but wasn't. Vera knew that ring. She'd put it on Mira's hand herself, six years ago, a birthday present from a mother who couldn't be present for birthdays. *For when you need to remember that someone loves you*, she'd said. *Even when they're not there.*

The finger was fresh. The blood at the severed end hadn't dried yet.

Underneath the straw, wrapped around the base of the finger, was a folded piece of paper. Vera's hands were steady as she unfolded it, which was wrong. Her hands should have been shaking. Instead they were perfectly, terrifyingly steady, the hands of someone who had done this before, who knew the exact temperature of horror and had learned to live inside it.

The message was written in the same hand as the letter on her counter—the slanting rightward script that couldn't seem to stay on the page. Three lines:

*She came willingly. She asked about you.*
*The Pale Tower. Come alone.*
*You have until the tide turns.*

Vera read it once. Twice. A third time to be certain.

Then she set the paper down and walked to the window and looked out at the Flotilla spread beneath her like a wound on the water. The tideglass generators hummed their constant low note. Somewhere a child was laughing, the sound thin and strange and distant. The world kept moving. It always kept moving, even when you wanted it to stop, even when you needed it to stop, even when the only thing you had left was the space between one breath and the next.

Mira had asked about her.

Vera closed her eyes. The image came anyway: her daughter, fifteen now, tall and dark-haired like she'd been at that age, walking into the Pale Tower with her chin raised and her questions ready. Walking in because someone had told her Vera was there. Walking in because someone had promised her answers.

Vera had told herself she was staying away for Mira's protection. She had told herself that her absence was a kind of love, a buffer between her daughter and the blood that had never stopped following her. She had told herself a lot of things in four years, and every single one of them had been a lie.

She opened her eyes. The letter from the Housing Office was still on the counter. Mira's handwriting, slanting hard to the right. *Mom, I get to come home.*

The Pale Tower. Come alone.

Vera turned away from the window and went to the chest at the foot of her bed. The lid was heavy, the hinges rusted from salt air, but it opened easily when she lifted it. Inside, wrapped in oilcloth and buried under a layer of fishing weights and net needles, were the things she'd promised herself she'd never use again.

The throwing knives came out first. Three of them, balanced for her grip, edges still sharp despite four years of disuse. She checked each one, running her thumb along the blade, feeling for imperfections the way she used to teach Mira to check for weak spots in a net. *Feel for what doesn't belong. The sea will show you if you know how to look.*

Next the sap—a length of leather cord with an iron weight at one end, the weight worn smooth from years of use and then four years of waiting. It fit her palm the way it always had, like it had been made for her hand specifically, which in a sense it had. Her first mentor had given it to her when she was twelve, a year after the flood took her parents and left her alone in a world that didn't care whether she lived or died.

The Covenant had found her six months later. They'd given her purpose when purpose was all she'd had.

She set the sap on the counter beside the box with Mira's finger.

The ring she picked up last. The opal was cloudy now, the play of light gone dull, but Vera could remember how it had looked when Mira first put it on—could remember her daughter's face, nine years old and trying so hard to be solemn, the ring loose on her thin finger. *I'll keep it safe*, Mira had said. *I'll keep it until you come back.*

Vera had come back. She had kept coming back, year after year, in secret, in the margins, in the spaces between Mira's life and her own. She had come back and she had never stayed and now Mira was in the Pale Tower asking about her, and Vera understood exactly what that meant.

The Covenant collected vessels. Children born with the capacity for bloodforge, for pulling essence from the living world and shaping it to deadly purpose. Mira was Vessel-class—Vera had known since the girl was three, had seen it in the way Mira's toys moved when she was angry, the way birds would land on her shoulder when she was sad. Vessel-class meant power. Vessel-class meant they would never let her go.

Vera had stolen evidence of the breeding program four years ago. She had sent it to the Dockward Council, to the Tidewell Monastery, to anyone who might listen. Nothing had happened. The Covenant was too deeply rooted, too protected, too necessary to Verath's economy for anyone to move against it. And Vera had been too much of a coward to go back, to finish what she'd started, to burn it all down before it could take her daughter.

Now Mira was asking about her in the Pale Tower. Mira was walking into the dragon's mouth on the strength of a lie about where her mother was hiding.

Vera pulled the stool out from under the counter and sat down. Her knees ached. Her hands ached. Everything ached in the way it had been aching for fifteen years, the accumulated debt of bloodforge use that no amount of time in the Flotilla could repay.

She should eat something. She should plan. She should do a lot of things that sensible people did when their children were kidnapped by the criminal syndicate they'd once worked for.

Instead she sat there, looking at the ring, and let herself feel it for the first time.

The grief was old. She'd been grieving since the day she left Mira with the neighbor in Verath's lower quarters, since the day she'd walked onto the Flotilla ferry and not looked back. That grief had been a companion for four years, a weight she'd learned to carry, a reminder that some things couldn't be fixed no matter how much you wanted to fix them.

But this was different. This was sharp where the other had been dull. This was a blade sliding between ribs, and it hurt in a way she hadn't let herself hurt in years.

*She came willingly. She asked about you.*

Mira had come looking for her. Mira had believed whatever story they'd told, had walked into the Pale Tower on the chance that her mother might be inside, might finally be ready to stop hiding.

And Vera had been here. Vera had been sitting at her counter with cold coffee and a letter that promised a future she should have known better than to trust. Vera had been waiting for the ferry, practicing words that didn't matter, planning a homecoming that was never going to be hers.

The ferry would arrive in three days. Mira wouldn't be on it.

Vera stood up. The throwing knives went into the sheaths along her forearms, hidden under the loose linen of her shirt. The sap went into her left pocket, the weight familiar against her hip. She picked up the ring—Mira's ring, the opal dull against her scarred palm—and slipped it onto the chain around her neck, where it lay cold against her sternum.

The letter on the counter, the one from the Housing Office, she left where it was. Someone would find it. Someone would know that Vera Salt, widow, net-mender, had been expecting her daughter.

The Pale Tower was south of Verath, built on a rocky outcrop that the floods hadn't reached. Three hours by fast boat. Longer if she had to go through the Undercroft, if the tunnels were compromised, if the Covenant had watchers in the places she used to consider safe.

Vera climbed down from her room to the small boat moored beneath, the one she used for fishing and supply runs and the long nights when sleep wouldn't come and she needed to feel the water under her. The engine caught on the first try, which was unusual—most things in the Flotilla required coaxing, repair, the constant maintenance of lives lived at the margins.

She didn't trust it. She trusted the knife along her right forearm and the sap in her pocket and the ring cold against her skin. She trusted the counting habit that kept her mind sharp, the awareness of exits that had never quite gone dormant. She trusted the stillness that came over her now, the silence that meant she was dangerous, the emptiness that the Covenant had carved into her years ago and that she'd never quite managed to fill.

The boat pulled away from the Flotilla, cutting through the dark water toward Verath's distant lights.

The Pale Tower. Come alone.

She had no intention of coming alone. But she had no intention of following their rules either.

Somewhere behind her, the Flotilla creaked and shifted in the current, a hundred broken vessels lashed together into something that almost resembled a community. Somewhere ahead, Mira was waiting in a tower built on blood and tideglass, asking questions about a mother who'd spent four years pretending she didn't exist.

Vera counted the lights of the city as the boat approached. Seventeen islands, the same as always. Seventeen points of darkness and illumination, the wealthy in their upper arches burning bright while the drowned quarters flickered below.

She would go to the Pale Tower. She would walk through whatever doors they opened and sit across from whatever handlers they'd assigned to her daughter. She would smile the way she used to smile, the one that didn't reach her eyes, and she would let them believe she was following instructions.

And then she would show them what retired meant.

The ring lay against her chest like a heartbeat she couldn't quite feel. Outside the boat, the water ran black and cold and deep, and somewhere in its depths the drowned places of the world kept their secrets.

Vera didn't look back.

The tide was turning.
